Metadata.Case_Number: Civil Appeals Nos 94 and 98 of 2016; Suit No 672 of 2013
Metadata.Coram: Chao Hick Tin JA, Andrew Phang Boon Leong JA, Judith Prakash JA
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Court of Appeal of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Goh Chan Peng

Facts.Text: From 2000-05-01, Goh Chan Peng held the position of director and chief executive officer of Beyonics Technology Ltd and Beyonics International Pte Ltd, and chief executive officer of the Beyonics Group.
On 2013-01-09, Goh Chan Peng tendered his resignation from his positions in the Beyonics Group with effect from 2013-04-30.
Between 2013-01-10 and 2013-04-30, Beyonics International Pte Ltd paid monthly salary totalling SGD 45,900 to Goh Chan Peng following his resignation.
At all material times, Goh Chan Peng was the beneficial owner of Wyser International Ltd and Wyser Capital Ltd, companies incorporated in the British Virgin Islands.

On 2012-04-05, Goh Chan Peng signed two agreements on behalf of Wyser International Ltd: one with Kodec Co Ltd and one with Nedec Co Ltd. Both agreements were backdated to 2011-11-24 and provided for payments to be made to Wyser International Ltd.
Between January 2012 and January 2013, the Beyonics Group supplied more than eight million e-coated baseplates to LND for finishing instead of finishing the baseplates itself.

The First Wyser Agreement provided for payment by Kodec of US$0.02 per e-coated baseplate for 'monthly sales and management support service' as part of Wyser-I's assistance in securing '6 million baseplates capacity business ... for the Seagate Brink 2H program'.
Mr Goh did not dispute that he had received US$200,000 under the Second Wyser Agreement.
In late June 2011, at his own request, Mr Goh was introduced to Stephen Hwang, CEO of the NedKo Group, who indicated that he wanted to discuss doing business with Seagate.

Between 4 and 7 September 2011, Stephen Hwang and Tony Lee visited factories belonging to the Beyonics Group in Thailand and Malaysia, and e-mails exchanged after this visit revealed that Mr Goh had given advice on surviving in the hard disk drive business and had agreed to provide the NedKo Group with certain data and information on the Beyonics PE Division.
On 30 September 2011, Stephen Hwang extended an invitation to Mr Goh to visit LND, which Mr Goh accepted.

On 4 October 2011, Tony Lee e-mailed Mr Goh expressing appreciation for Mr Goh's intended visit to the NedKo factories on about 10 November 2011 and stated that he saw it as an opportunity for parties to engage in 'further talks for co-operation and collaboration'.

Around mid-October 2011, in communications with Seagate, Mr Goh under-represented the Beyonics Group's capacity to produce baseplates and showed little commitment and willingness to support Seagate; Mr Goh informed Billy Chua that the Beyonics Group was only able to continue with the supply of three million Brinks 2H baseplates per quarter despite Billy Chua having indicated that he required a commitment for more than four million baseplates per quarter.

On 26 October 2011, Tony Lee e-mailed Mr Goh to inform him about his forthcoming meeting with Seagate on 27 October 2011, and stated that the issues to be discussed might include a 'joint operation' of his Changsu factory and asked to meet Mr Goh on 28 October 2011.

On 27 October 2011, ten minutes before Seagate was to meet Tony Lee for the first time, Mr Goh sent an e-mail to Billy Chua claiming that '... due to having major losses in PE division; our investment is limited and we could only exercise limited investment strategy to help the current demand faced by our customers'.
On 28 October 2011, Mr Goh and Tony Lee met, and agreed to the proposed alliance, even though the parties had not discussed key terms such as pricing.

On 10 November 2011, Mr Goh visited LND's factory. The B-N Alliance was discussed during the visit. Tony Lee also testified that Mr Goh then suggested making improvements to enable the NedKo Group to achieve the necessary qualification to do Second Stage Work for Seagate. During the discussion, Tony Lee suggested that Mr Goh be compensated for his consultancy services to the NedKo Group.

On 18 November 2011, Mr Goh met Seagate concerning the B-N Alliance, and presented slides ('the November 2011 Slides') that pushed for the alliance as well as for a US$2.5m grant from Seagate for the NedKo Group. At the same meeting, he indicated that the PE Division of the Beyonics Group faced a shortage of machines and equipment.
On 24 November 2011, Seagate confirmed the B-N Alliance in a meeting between representatives of Seagate, the Beyonics Group and the NedKo Group at the Seagate office.
On 10 January 2012, the BAP-LND Contract formalised the B-N Alliance.

On 18 November 2011, Mr Goh used the presentation slides to push for the US$2.5m grant from Seagate. The judgment also states that he initiated the idea of procuring a grant from Seagate, strategised on how to secure it and assisted the NedKo Group in obtaining it.
At the meeting on 18 November 2011, Mr Goh indicated that the PE Division of the Beyonics Group faced a shortage of machines and equipment.
In an e-mail dated 20 February 2012, Tony Lee confirmed that Mr Goh had promised earlier that the NedKo Group would be regarded as a 'top priority as agreed'.
In another e-mail, dated 30 March 2012, Mr Goh promised Tony Lee the right of first refusal with regard to the sale of BTEC.

On 3 January 2012, Mr Goh quoted a 'friend price' of US$40m to Tony Lee. This quotation was made without the knowledge of the other directors of BTL and the price offered was considerably below the figure of US$50m that the Beyonics Group had asked from another interested buyer.
Beyonics-I paid $99,598.80 for wine purchases made between June 2006 and December 2012.
Beyonics-I paid HK$38,400 spent on a piece of medical equipment known as an impulse massager in May 2012.
Beyonics-I paid $1,464.65 spent on course fees for courses attended by Mr Goh's daughter between July and December 2011.

Beyonics-I paid $8,225 being the balance of $10,329 paid from petty cash for treatments administered to Mr Goh by a Japanese practitioner of alternative healthcare during the period from 26 July 2012 to 2 January 2013.
Beyonics-I paid $1,679 being the cost of a camera lens purchased on 2 November 2012.
Beyonics-I paid $16,000 being the cost of ten fountain pens purchased on 7 December 2011.

In early October 2011, the Beyonics Group was considering the possibility of divesting itself of the PE Division, primarily by selling BTEC, the Chinese subsidiary that manufactured baseplates. The evidence indicated that Mr Goh favoured the NedKo Group as a possible purchaser of BTEC.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, before the dispute, the conduct and decisions of Goh Chan Peng in relation to the Beyonics Group's dealings with Seagate and the NedKo Group, his personal and Wyser-related agreements with Nedec and Kodec, and his use of Beyonics International Pte Ltd funds for various purchases and post-resignation salary, corresponded with or diverged from the Beyonics Group's commercial and financial interests.
Party_Details.Rule: Company directors and chief executive officers are generally manage the company's business in the company's commercial interests, avoid placing their personal financial arrangements in situations that intersect with company business without full prior authorisation, and ensure that company funds are applied for purposes reasonably connected to the company's operations and agreed remuneration structures.
Party_Details.Application: The pre-dispute record shows that while serving as director and chief executive officer of Beyonics Technology Ltd and Beyonics International Pte Ltd, Goh Chan Peng initiated and developed contacts with the NedKo Group, participated in factory visits, and supported a collaboration structure under which first stage work remained with the Beyonics Group and second stage work and a tooling grant were directed to NedKo Group entities. He then caused Wyser International Ltd, which he beneficially owned, to enter into agreements with Nedec and Kodec that provided for payments to Wyser International Ltd linked to Seagate-related baseplate volumes and a tooling grant, while he continued to hold his positions in the Beyonics Group. During the same period, he was involved in communications about a potential sale of BTEC to the NedKo Group on terms he discussed directly with NedKo representatives. Separately, Beyonics International Pte Ltd funds were used over several years for wine purchases, a medical device, alternative healthcare treatments for him, course fees for his daughter, a camera lens kept by him, and high-value fountain pens, and he received salary after tendering his resignation pursuant to an arrangement recorded in his 2013-01-09 notice. These events created a factual pattern in which his personal and Wyser-related financial arrangements, and his directions on company expenditure and collaboration structures, intersected with the Beyonics Group's baseplate business and internal financial policies.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The Court of Appeal accepted the High Court's core findings that Goh Chan Peng, while director and CEO of Beyonics Technology Ltd and Beyonics International Pte Ltd and CEO of the Beyonics Group, acted in breach of his fiduciary duties. The court applied the combined subjective and objective test for the duty to act bona fide in the best interests of the company, and the Charterbridge standard, and held that his conduct was not objectively in Beyonics Technology Ltd's interests. It found that the Beyonics Group had sufficient capacity to perform the second stage work that was diverted to the NedKo Group, and that Goh under-represented capacity to Seagate and promoted the Beyonics–NedKo alliance and the Seagate grant in a way that favoured the NedKo Group. The court held that the Wyser agreements were secret profit arrangements and characterised the payments as bribes or unauthorised commissions obtained by reason of his fiduciary office, engaging both the no-conflict and no-profit rules. It rejected his argument that the payments were consultancy fees not received qua director and found there was no full and frank disclosure or informed consent by the board. The court also relied on his deletion of emails and his unreliable and evasive evidence to reject his claim of honest belief. It declined to grant relief under section 391 of the Companies Act, holding that his breaches were egregious and not honest or reasonable. On loss, the court held that the diversion loss and total loss were suffered by Beyonics Asia Pacific Limited, which contracted with and invoiced Seagate, and not by Beyonics Technology Ltd. It rejected the single economic entity approach and held that consolidated accounts and group control did not displace separate legal personality. Accordingly, it set aside the awards to Beyonics Technology Ltd for diversion loss and total loss. For Beyonics International Pte Ltd, the court upheld the High Court's orders that various expenses were unjustified, applying the Lee Behrens test and the shifting of evidential burden as described in John While Springs. It agreed that the medical device, alternative healthcare treatments, course fees for his daughter, and camera lens were not reasonably incidental to the business or for the company's benefit, and that the wine purchases were unjustified given the scale, lack of records, and his refusal to cooperate with internal investigation. It disagreed only on the fountain pens, treating them as acceptable staff and director gifts and reducing the unjustified expenses accordingly. On salary, the court held that although breaches of fiduciary duty do not by themselves justify withholding contractually due salary, the specific post-resignation salary arrangement lacked consideration because the shortened notice period was requested by Goh and there was no legal entitlement to salary in lieu of notice or requested forbearance to sue. It therefore upheld recovery of the post-resignation salary on that basis. The court dismissed Goh's appeal in part, maintaining liability for the Wyser payments and unjustified expenses and salary, and dismissed Beyonics Technology Ltd's cross-appeal on quantification as academic after finding that Beyonics Technology Ltd could not claim the loss of profits.

Metadata.Case_Number: Civil Appeals Nos 94 and 98 of 2016; Suit No 672 of 2013
Metadata.Coram: Chao Hick Tin JA, Andrew Phang Boon Leong JA, Judith Prakash JA
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Court of Appeal of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Wyser International Ltd

Facts.Text: Wyser International Ltd is a company incorporated in the British Virgin Islands and at all material times was beneficially owned by Goh Chan Peng.

On 2012-04-05, Wyser International Ltd, through Goh Chan Peng, entered into a written agreement with Kodec Co Ltd that was dated 2011-11-24 and provided for payment of US$0.02 per e-coated baseplate for monthly sales and management support services related to securing six million baseplates capacity business for the Seagate Brinks 2H programme.

On 2012-04-05, Wyser International Ltd, through Goh Chan Peng, entered into a written agreement with Nedec Co Ltd that was dated 2011-11-24 and provided for a payment of US$500,000 to Wyser International Ltd for assistance in securing a US$2.5 million grant funded by Seagate.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, before the dispute, Wyser International Ltd's agreements with Nedec Co Ltd and Kodec Co Ltd, and the resulting receipt of payments linked to Seagate-related baseplate volumes and a tooling grant while its beneficial owner was serving as director and chief executive officer of the Beyonics Group, corresponded with or diverged from the commercial interests and arrangements of the Beyonics Group.
Party_Details.Rule: A company that enters into agreements to receive payments connected to business opportunities or grants associated with another company, where its controller simultaneously holds senior management positions in that other company, may be ensure that such arrangements are transparent to and authorised by the affected company and do not create undisclosed intersections between the controller's personal or related-entity income and the other company's business.
Party_Details.Application: Wyser International Ltd, beneficially owned by Goh Chan Peng, entered into backdated agreements with Kodec Co Ltd and Nedec Co Ltd that provided for payments to Wyser International Ltd based on e-coated baseplate volumes for Seagate's Brinks 2H programme and for assistance in securing a US$2.5 million Seagate-funded grant. These agreements were concluded while Goh Chan Peng was still director and chief executive officer of Beyonics Technology Ltd and Beyonics International Pte Ltd and chief executive officer of the Beyonics Group, and while the Beyonics Group was collaborating with the NedKo Group on Seagate baseplate production. The payments to Wyser International Ltd were therefore factually connected to the same Seagate-related business in which the Beyonics Group was involved, creating a situation where a related entity of the Beyonics Group's chief executive officer received income tied to that business.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The Court of Appeal treated Wyser International Ltd as the vehicle through which Goh Chan Peng received unauthorised profits arising from his fiduciary position in the Beyonics Group. It held that the Wyser agreements with Nedec and Kodec were secret profit arrangements and that the payments constituted bribes or unauthorised commissions obtained by reason of Goh's fiduciary office. The court found that Wyser International Ltd knowingly received these payments in circumstances where they were linked to the diversion of second stage work and the procurement of the Seagate grant, and that Wyser International Ltd was jointly and severally liable with Goh to account for the sums of US$200,000 and US$166,544.02. The court rejected arguments that the payments were legitimate consultancy fees or that they were not received qua director, and emphasised that the opportunity arose from Goh's role in the Beyonics Group. It therefore upheld the High Court's orders requiring Wyser International Ltd to disgorge the Wyser payments, while setting aside only the separate awards for diversion loss and total loss that had been made in favour of Beyonics Technology Ltd.

Metadata.Case_Number: Civil Appeals Nos 94 and 98 of 2016; Suit No 672 of 2013
Metadata.Coram: Chao Hick Tin JA, Andrew Phang Boon Leong JA, Judith Prakash JA
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Court of Appeal of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Beyonics Technology Ltd

Facts.Text: From 1987, the precision engineering division of the Beyonics Group, which included Beyonics Technology Ltd and its subsidiaries, supplied baseplates for hard disk drives to Seagate Technology International.
From 2000-05-01, Beyonics Technology Ltd employed Goh Chan Peng as a director and chief executive officer.
In early October 2011, the Beyonics Group was considering the possibility of divesting itself of the PE Division, primarily by selling BTEC, the Chinese subsidiary that manufactured baseplates.

On 10 January 2012, the BAP-LND Contract formalised the B-N Alliance. It was agreed that for Seagate's Brinks 2H programme, BTEC would complete the First Stage Work and supply e-coated baseplates to LND, and LND would then perform the Second Stage Work and sell the finished baseplates to Seagate.
Between January 2012 and January 2013, the Beyonics Group supplied more than eight million e-coated baseplates to LND for finishing instead of finishing the baseplates itself.
The grant, in the sum of US$2.5m, was to cover the NedKo Group's 'new tooling expenses' needed to manufacture baseplates for Seagate.
From 2012 onwards, the volume of the Beyonics Group's sales of baseplates to Seagate declined steadily.
By the end of its financial year 2014, the Beyonics Group had ceased supplying baseplates to Seagate.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, before the dispute, the collaboration structure with the NedKo Group, the supply of e-coated baseplates to LND, the Seagate tooling grant to the NedKo Group, and the subsequent decline and cessation of Seagate baseplate business were associated with a loss of profits for Beyonics Technology Ltd as distinct from its subsidiary Beyonics Asia Pacific Limited.
Party_Details.Rule: In a corporate group, each incorporated company is treated as a separate legal entity, and loss of revenue or profit arising from contracts performed and invoiced by a particular subsidiary is generally attributed to that subsidiary rather than to the holding company, notwithstanding consolidated financial reporting or group control.
Party_Details.Application: The precision engineering division of the Beyonics Group supplied baseplates to Seagate through a structure in which Beyonics Asia Pacific Limited contracted with Seagate and recognised the revenue, while manufacturing work was carried out by subsidiaries such as BTEC. After the 2012 contract between Beyonics Asia Pacific Limited and LND, second stage work for Seagate's Brinks 2H programme was performed by LND, and more than eight million e-coated baseplates were supplied to LND instead of being finished within the Beyonics Group. The US$2.5 million tooling grant from Seagate was paid to the NedKo Group. From 2012, the Beyonics Group's sales to Seagate declined and had ceased by the end of financial year 2014. The financial evidence at trial showed that the diversion and total loss calculations were based on profits that would have been recognised in Beyonics Asia Pacific Limited's accounts, and there was no separate quantification of loss at the Beyonics Technology Ltd level.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The Court of Appeal held that Beyonics Technology Ltd could not recover the diversion loss and total loss claimed against Goh Chan Peng because those losses were suffered by Beyonics Asia Pacific Limited, which contracted with and invoiced Seagate, and not by Beyonics Technology Ltd. The court rejected the High Court's reliance on the single economic entity concept and on the fact that Beyonics Technology Ltd prepared consolidated accounts and controlled its subsidiaries. It reaffirmed the doctrine of separate legal personality, citing Lew Syn Pau and Manuchar Steel, and held that consolidated financial statements do not convert a subsidiary's profits into the holding company's profits. The court found that the expert evidence showed the diversion and total loss were calculated on a consolidated or group basis but in substance reflected losses in Beyonics Asia Pacific Limited. It concluded that Beyonics Technology Ltd had not proved a direct loss of profits and therefore set aside the awards of US$2,970,559 for diversion loss and US$4,793,591 for total loss. The court noted that while this outcome was harsh given Goh's serious breaches, it could not disregard corporate separateness in the absence of veil-piercing grounds. Beyonics Technology Ltd's cross-appeal in CA 98 on the quantification period for total loss was dismissed as academic once its entitlement to claim the loss was rejected.

Metadata.Case_Number: Civil Appeals Nos 94 and 98 of 2016; Suit No 672 of 2013
Metadata.Coram: Chao Hick Tin JA, Andrew Phang Boon Leong JA, Judith Prakash JA
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Court of Appeal of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Beyonics International Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: On 31 August 2005, Goh Chan Peng's employment agreement with Beyonics International Pte Ltd contained a contractual notice provision allowing either party to terminate the employment by giving six months' written notice.
From 26 July 2012 to 2 January 2013, Beyonics International Pte Ltd paid $10,329 from petty cash for treatments administered to Mr Goh by a Japanese practitioner of alternative healthcare.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, before the dispute, the use of Beyonics International Pte Ltd funds for wine, a medical device, alternative healthcare treatments, course fees for the chief executive officer's daughter, a camera lens, fountain pens, and post-resignation salary corresponded with or diverged from the company's business purposes and agreed remuneration terms.
Party_Details.Rule: Company expenditure by senior management is generally be reasonably incidental to the carrying on of the company's business, undertaken in good faith, and directed to the benefit or prosperity of the company, and salary payments are typically governed by the terms of the employment contract and any subsequent variation supported by consideration.
Party_Details.Application: Beyonics International Pte Ltd's records , over several years, funds were used for wine purchases, a medical device, alternative healthcare treatments for the chief executive officer, course fees for his daughter, a camera lens kept by him, and high-value fountain pens for directors and senior personnel. The company's human resources manual limited reimbursement for non-conventional medical treatments, and there was no contemporaneous documentation linking the medical device, alternative treatments, or camera lens to specific corporate projects or policies. The wine purchases were not tracked to particular corporate events or recipients, although some wine was used at company functions. The fountain pens were purchased as gifts for directors and staff. Separately, the 2005 employment agreement required six months' notice for termination, and the 2013-01-09 resignation arrangement shortened the notice period while providing for salary until 2013-04-30, without evidence of additional consideration from the employee beyond the resignation itself.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The Court of Appeal upheld the High Court's conclusion that most of the impugned expenses and the post-resignation salary were recoverable from Goh Chan Peng. Applying the Lee Behrens test, it agreed that the impulse massager, alternative healthcare treatments, course fees for his daughter, and the camera lens were not reasonably incidental to Beyonics International Pte Ltd's business or for its benefit, and that the evidential burden had shifted to Goh to justify them, which he failed to do. For the wine purchases, the court acknowledged the lack of direct proof of misappropriation but considered the scale of expenditure, absence of proper records, and Goh's refusal to cooperate with internal investigations sufficient to uphold the finding that the expenses were unjustified. It disagreed with the High Court only on the fountain pens, holding that they could reasonably be treated as corporate gifts for directors and staff and reducing the unjustified expenses by SGD 16,000. On salary, the court distinguished John While Springs and applied Schonk, holding that breaches of fiduciary duty do not by themselves negate contractual salary entitlements. However, it found that the shortened notice arrangement in the 2013-01-09 resignation letter lacked consideration because Goh had no contractual or common law right to salary in lieu of notice and there was no requested forbearance to sue. The court therefore affirmed recovery of the SGD 45,900 post-resignation salary as money paid under an agreement unsupported by consideration, while maintaining the overall judgment in favour of Beyonics International Pte Ltd for unjustified expenses and salary, subject to the reduction for the fountain pens.
